Appendix A: <Evidence of Requirements Elicitation Artefacts>

  1. Introduction

  1. Purpose

        This SRS document provides the functional and non-functional requirements of GameOn Sports Teams Matching Application. it serves as a guideline for the developer, tester, and stakeholders to make sure that the system will satisfy user needs to connect sports enthusiasts.

  1. Scope

  The software product is a Website that enables users to:

  • Create user profiles including their sport preferences & skills levels
  • Create and join sports teams based on skill level and location
  • Search for nearby players/teams using geolocation
  • Manae team information and communications
  • Receive AI-powered training tips

         

Exclusions:

  • Direct venue booking functionality
  • Payment processing systems

  1. Definitions, Acronyms and Abbreviation

AI: Artificial Intelligence

API: Application Programming Interface

GPS: Global Positioning System

JWT: JSON Web Token

UI/UX: User Interface/User Experience

  1. Overview

  The present SRS paper offers a thorough specification of the GameOn Sports Teams Matching Application, a web-developed application created in the effort to link sports fans with appropriate teams according to their level of competence, location, and preferences. The functional requirements defined in the document entail user registration, forming teams and managing them, geolocation-based matching, and AI-driven training suggestions.

  1. Specific Requirements

  1. User characteristics

    Two primary users and an administrator will use the GameOn Sports Team Matching to ensure a safe environment. A universal design and a smooth interaction to accommodate users technical skills, age and knowledge differences.

        2.1.1 Casual players/Competitive

  • Demographics : Ages 16-60 , varying fitness levels.
  • Technical skills :  basic smartphone/web literacy, may struggle advanced filtering
  • Behaviour : Would like to be matched with a team in less than 3 clicks. Demands a clear picture of the minimum level of skills needed, profiles of team members, event venue. In Case of creating a team and becoming a hoste, Requires detailed team customization, accepts and rejects join requests by other users, Able to review users profile.

2.1. Admin

  • Technical skills  : Basic familiarity with admin dashboards, no specialized training required.

  • Behaviour :

  • Delete User Accounts: Action ; Permanently remove fraudulent/inactive accounts.
  • Handle User Reports: Action: Review reports (e.g., "Fake profile," "Inappropriate content").
  • Delete Teams: Remove teams violating guidelines (e.g., spam, fake listings).

  1. System Features 

The GameOn sports team matching is a website that connects sport enthusiasts with the most suitable teams to play their favorite sports, ensuring optimal match based on the skill level and availability. The website simplifies the process of gathering and managing team members. The website enables team customization allowing only specific skill levels to join the team.

        The GameOn website implements geographic location services(GPS) that filters out the nearby teams. Each user should enable their location access permission to the website. The website allows users to filter and search specific descriptions (e.g.  sports type, skill level, age, gender, location, competitive/friendly games ) of the team they want to join.

        The system combines third-party NLP AI models (e.g. DeepSeek API) to provide spot-on performance enhancing tips. Users enter their sports and their current problems (e.g., “improve free kicks in football“ ), and Then AI comes up with action plans that the user can follow to overcome these problems.

Users within  the system will be divided into two  users. While the end user should have the ability to be both a team host and a player that participates into other formed teams. Admin users moderates the users activities and reviews reports, while having the ability to delete suspicious teams and users.  

  1. System Features 

Figure 1.0: Use Case Diagram for <GameOn sports team matching>

 

Table 1.0: Description of Module and Functions for <GameOn sports team matching>

Function

Description

UC01 – Register User

Allows new users to create an account

UC02 – Edit User Profile

Allows users to modify their profile information

UC03 – Create Game

User creates one Game

UC04 – Manage Game

Control hub for Game owners to control and customize owned Games

UC05 – Edit Game Info

Modify owned Game details

UC06 – Remove Game Members

Eject members from Game

UC07 – Delete Own Game

Disband owned Game

UC08 – Accept Join Requests

Game owner reviews and accepts player join requests

UC09 – Send Join Request

Player requests to join existing Games

UC10 – Search Games

Find Games based on criteria like location, sport, skill level

UC11 – View Games Info

Display detailed information about available Game

UC12 – Leave Game

Player exits from joined Game

UC13 – Report Game

Report inappropriate Game behavior or content

UC14 –

Get AI Performance Tips

Receive personalized training recommendations based on sport and skill level

UC15 – Delete Any Game

Remove Game violating platform guidelines

UC16 – View Reports

Review user-submitted reports about Games

UC17- View Notifications

notify user on changes on the Game status

  1. Use Case Details        

  1. UC01: Use Case <Register Account>

Use Case ID

UC01

Use Case Name

Register Account

Description

Allows new users to create an account

Actor(s)

<Player>

Pre-condition(s)

none

Normal Flow(s)- NF

1. User enters email and password

2. System validates and creates account

3. User selects preferred sports and sets respective skills.

Alternative Flow(s) - AF

none

Exception Flow(s) - EF

1. Email already exists - System displays error message

2. Invalid email format - System prompts for valid email

3. Password doesn't meet requirements - System displays password criteria

Post-condition

New user profile created.

 

Table 1.1: Use Case Description for <GameOn sports team matching>

 

Figure 2.1: Sequence Diagram for <UC01>

  1. UC02: Use Case <Edit Profile>

Use Case ID

UC02

Use Case Name

Edit Profile

Description

Allows users to modify their profile information

Actor(s)

<Player>

Pre-condition(s)

User must be logged in

Normal Flow(s)- NF

  1. User navigates to the "Edit Profile" section
  2. User updates desired profile fields (e.g., name, location, preferred sports, skill level of each sport)
  3. User saves the changes
  4. System updates the user's profile information

Alternative Flow(s) - AF

none

Exception

Flow(s) - EF

1. Invalid data format - System displays validation errors

2. System error during save - System displays error message and retains original data

Post-condition

User's profile information is updated

Use Case ID

UC03

Use Case Name

Create Games

Description

User creates one primary Games

Actor(s)

<Player>

Pre-condition(s)

User is logged in

Normal Flow(s)- NF

  1.  User selects "Create Game" option
  2. User enters Game details (name, sport, location, skill level, description)
  3. System validates Game Information
  4. System creates Game with user as owner

Alternative Flow(s) - AF

none

Exception

Flow(s) - EF

New game created with user as game host and two empty team slots

Team is listed on the Public teams list

  1. UC08: Use Case <Accept join Requests>

Table 1.8: Use Case Description for <GameOn sports team matching>

Use Case ID

UC08

Use Case Name

Accept join Requests

Description

Game owner reviews and accepts player join requests

     Actor(s)

<Player>

Pre-condition(s)

User must own the Game

Normal Flow(s)- NF

  1. User views pending join requests
  2. User reviews player profiles and request details
  3. User selects approve or reject for each request
  4.  System processes decisions

Alternative Flow(s) - AF

 User defers decision for later review

Exception

Flow(s) - EF

 Game is full - System prevents accepting new members

Request no longer valid - System removes invalid requests

Post-condition

Join requests processed and players notified

Figure 2.8: Sequence Diagram for <UC08-Accept join Requests>

  1. UC09: Use Case <Send Join Request>

Table 1.9 : Use Case Description for <GameOn sports team matching>

Use Case ID

UC09

Use Case Name

Send Join Request

Description

Player requests to join existing Game

     Actor(s)

<Player>

Pre-condition(s)

User must be logged in and not already a member of the target Game

Normal Flow(s)- NF

  1. User finds desired Game
  2. User views Game details
  3.  User clicks "Request to Join"
  4. User adds optional message
  5.  System sends request to Game owner

Alternative Flow(s) - AF

non

Exception

Flow(s) - EF

  1. Already a member - System prevents duplicate request
  2. Previous request pending - System displays existing request status
  3.  Game not accepting members - System displays message

Post-condition

Join request sent to Game owner

Figure 2.9: Sequence Diagram for <UC09>

Use Case ID

UC010

Use Case Name

Search Games

Description

Find Game based on criteria like location, sport, skill level

     Actor(s)

<Player>

Pre-condition

User must be logged in

Normal Flow(s)- NF

  1. User accesses Game search interface
  2.  User enters search criteria (sport, location, skill level, keywords)
  3.  System processes search query
  4.  System displays matching Games with basic information

Alternative Flow(s) - AF

User searches without filters to view all Games

Exception

Flow(s) - EF

  1. No teams found - System displays "no results" message
  2. Invalid search criteria - System prompts for valid input

Post-condition

Join requests processed and players notified

<Player>

user must be logged in .

  2. System displays detailed game information (description, members, requirements, location, schedule)

Use Case ID

Leave Game

Description

Player exits from a joined game

<Player>

Pre-condition(s)

User is participating in a current game

Normal Flow(s)- NF

  1. User accesses game participation page
  2.  User selects "Leave Game" option
  3.  System prompts for confirmation
  4.  User confirms departure
  5.  System removes user from game and frees up team slot

Alternative Flow(s) - AF

 User cancel the leaving process

Exception

Flow(s) - EF

  1. User is game host - System requires game deletion or host transfer System error
  2. System displays error message

Post-condition

User removed from game and team slot becomes available

Use Case ID

UC15

Use Case Name

Delete Any Game

Description

Remove games violating platform guidelines

     Actor(s)

<Admin>

Pre-condition(s)

Admin must be logged in

Normal Flow(s)- NF

  1. Administrator searches for or selects game to delete
  2. Administrator reviews game information and violation details
  3.  Administrator confirms game deletion
  4. System removes all participants and deletes game
  5. System notifies affected users

Alternative Flow(s) - AF

non

Exception

Flow(s) - EF

  1.  Game not found - System displays error message
  2.  System error during deletion

Game permanently deleted and users notified

  1. UC016: Use Case <View Reports>

Table 1.16: Use Case Description for <GameOn sports team matching>

Use Case ID

UC16

Use Case Name

View Reports

Description

Review user-submitted reports about games

     Actor(s)

<Administrator>

Pre-condition(s)

Admin must be logged in

Normal Flow(s)- NF

  1.  Administrator accesses reports dashboard
  2. System displays list of pending reports
  3.  Administrator selects report to review
  4.  System displays report details, evidence, and involved parties
  5. Administrator can take action or mark as resolved

Alternative Flow(s) - AF

non

Exception

Flow(s) - EF

1. No reports available - System displays empty state

2. Report details unavailable - System displays error message

  1. UC017: Use Case <View Notifications>

Table 1.17: Use Case Description for <GameOn sports team matching>

Use Case ID

UC17

Use Case Name

View Notifications

Description

User views system notifications (e.g., game acceptance, game deletion, join requests, etc.).

     Actor(s)

<User>

Pre-condition(s)

Admin must be logged in

Normal Flow(s)- NF

  1. User opens the notifications page.
  2. System retrieves and displays a list of unread notifications.
  3. User selects a notification to view details.
  4. System marks the notification as read.
  5. System displays the notification content (e.g., "Your join request was accepted").

Alternative Flow(s) - AF

non

Exception

Flow(s) - EF

1. No Notifications available - System displays empty state

Post-condition

Notifications reviewed

Figure 2.17: Sequence Diagram for <UC17>

  1. Performance and Other Requirements

 Due to the scale of the PSM the aim of the functional requirements is to focus

        1.4.1 System Attributes

Usability

  • Easy Navigation: The user must be able to join and find teams in 3 clicks as mentioned in user requirements
  • Responsive Design: The web application should be responsive in desktop and mobile browsers
  • Intuitive Interface: The profile creation and the team search should be made on the intuitive forms with understandable labels
  • Error messages: System should provide good error messages in case the user does it wrongly

Reliability

  • Basic Functionality: The most essential functions (registration, creation of teams, search, join requests) are to remain stable
  • Error handling: System should not break down because of some common errors but it should gracefully handle it.
  • Data Persistence: Profiles of the users and information about the teams have to be stored properly in the database

Maintainability

  • Clean Code: The code must be structured by usage of descriptive names of variables and simple comments
  • Simple Architecture: Future development and modification should be simple and familiar, so use regular MVC or Mirco-Service based Arch.
  • Version Control: All changes in code that are marked in Git

Portability

  • Cross Browser Compatibility: The application must run on Chrome, Firefox and Safari

1.4.2 Other Requirements

Security

  1. Basic authentication:Password hash based secure logins
  2. Input validation: preventing common security attacks(SQL injections)
  3. Session Management:session control of user log in status

Safety

  • User Reporting: Simple reporting mechanism for inappropriate behavior

  1. Design Constraints

1.5.1 Technical Constraints

  • Development Platform: web-based application with the standard web technologies (HTML, CSS, JavaScript)
  • Database: Lean Database Solution (MONGODB, MonogDb Atlas )
  • Framework: employ commonly known web framework (React.js, Node.js, Express.js)
  • Hosting: Deploy on free or low-cost hosting platform (Heroku, Netlify, or university server)

Resource Constraints

  • Development Time: Project should be finished under the timeline of the academic semester
  • Budget: Free or minimal cost solutions only (free APIs, free hosting tiers)-
  • Third-Party Services: Limited to free tier of AI API (DeepSeek) for training recommendations
  • Testing Environment: Limited to personal devices and volunteer testers

Skill Constraints

  • Technology Stack: Use technologies within developer's current skill level or learning capacity
  • Complexity: Features should be implementable by single developer within project timeframe
  • API Integration: Limit to one external API (AI service) to manage complexity

Scope Constraints

  • Geographic Coverage: Initially focus on local area or university campus for testing
  • User Base: Designed for small user base (university students/local community)
  • Feature Set: Core features only - advanced features can be listed as future enhancements

Development Constraints

  • Documentation: Focus on essential documentation for project submission
  • Testing: Manual testing with basic test cases, automated testing optional
  • Version Control: Regular commits to Git repository for project tracking
  • Code Quality: Emphasis on working functionality over perfect optimiz

  APPENDIX C  Software Design Documentation

(SDS)

SECJ 3032: Software Engineering FYP1
Semester 01, 2024/2025


Software Design Specification (SDS)

GameOn Sports team matching

1.0

Prepared by: Ali Mohamed Fathy Abdelkader Elbermawy

  1. Revision Page
  1. Overview

        The following SDS document will give the complete design specification of the GameOn Sports Team Matching Application, a web-based application that helps sports enthusiasts find the right teams according to their skill level, location, and preferences. The document describes the system architecture, component design, data structures, user interfaces, and implementation details that are required to be developed.

  1. Issuing Organization / Team

Faculty of computing ; University Technology Malaysia

Course: SECJ 3032 Software Engineering FYP1

  1. Authorship / Project Team Members

Name

Role

Ali Mohamed Fathy Abdelkader Elbermawy

Developer/Designer/Developer

.

ISSUE DATE

DETAILS OF REVISION

01

May

Initial Submission

Note:

This Software Design Descriptions (SDD template is adapted from IEEE Recommended Practice based on Software Design Descriptions (SDD) (IEEE Std. 1016­1998 1), that are simplified and customized to meet the need of SECJ3203 FYP1 SE course at Faculty of Computing, UTM. Examples of models are from Arlow and Neustadt (2002) and other sources stated accordingly.

  1. Introduction

  1. Purpose

This SDS explains the architecture and implementation strategy of the detailed design of the GameOn Sports Team Matching Application. It is a complete guide to developers, testers, and technical stakeholders of the system implementation. The paper outlines the internal composition of the system, the interaction between the components, the data management approach and the interface design to be used to guarantee effective development and implementation.

  1. Scope

This system aims to provide the following features :

  • Develop detailed user profiles including sport preferences and skill levels
  • Design and organize sports Games with requirements that can be customized
  • Find and join teams according to the location, level of skill and type of sport
  • Get training suggestions on how to improve performance using AI
  • Report unsuitable content and control team communications
  • Content moderation and user management administrative control

The system does not incorporate direct venue booking features and payment processing systems, and its main feature is team matching and coordination services.

  1. Context

This standard is targeted at technical and managerial stakeholders that prepare and use SDS. It helps designers to select, organize and present design information. In the case of the GameOn Sports Team Matching system, this document is the main source of reference to; Software developer who executes the system components, Design of test cases through quality assurance principles, stakeholders including the owner and the end-users and Design decisions assessed by academic supervisors

  1. Summary

    This SDS document is structured into some major sections which include design ,stakeholders, various architectural perspectives such as context, composition, logical, information, interface, structure, interaction, state dynamic and algorithm perspectives. These perspectives deal with certain design issues and give detailed design views in the form of proper diagrams and specifications. The document ends with detailed user interface design specifications and appendices.

  1. References

  • DeepSeek. (2024). DeepSeek NLP API documentation.
    https://deepseek.com/nlp-api
  • GameOn Sports Team Matching - Software Requirements Specification (SRS) v1.0.
  • Google. (2024). Google Maps Platform documentation. Google Cloud.
    https://cloud.google.com/maps-platform
  • Google Developers. (n.d.). Why speed matters. web.dev.
    https://web.dev/why-speed-matters/
  • IEEE Standard for Software Design Descriptions (IEEE Std. 1016-1998).
  • Krug, S. (2000). Don’t make me think: A common sense approach to web usability (2nd ed.). New Riders.
  • MongoDB Inc. (2024). MongoDB documentation.
    https://www.mongodb.com/docs/
  • Nielsen, J. (1994). Usability engineering. Morgan Kaufmann.
  • Nielsen Norman Group. (1994). 10 usability heuristics for user interface design.
    https://www.nngroup.com/articles/ten-usability-heuristics/
  • Nielsen Norman Group. (2014). Response times: The 3 important limits.
    https://www.nngroup.com/articles/response-times-3-important-limits/
  • Node.js Foundation. (2023). Node.js documentation.
    https://nodejs.org/docs/latest/api/
  • Shneiderman, B. (1998). Designing the user interface: Strategies for effective human-computer interaction (3rd ed.). Pearson.
  • Smashing Magazine. (2017). Designing efficient web forms: Structure, inputs, labels and actions.
    https://www.smashingmagazine.com/2017/06/designing-efficient-web-forms/
  • Vercel. (2024). Next.js documentation.
    https://nextjs.org/docs

  1. Glossary

AI: Artificial Intelligence

API: Application Programming Interface

GPS: Global Positioning System

JWT: JSON Web Token

UI/UX: User Interface/User Experience

  1. Design Body

  1. Design Stakeholders and Their Concerns

The following stakeholders have identified for the GameOn system:

Stakeholder

Concerns

Developer (Ali)

Implementation feasibility, technology stack, maintainability

Owner ( Mohamed Elber )

Usability, performance, Location Security Concerns  feature completeness

End Users

Usability, performance, Location Security Concerns  feature completeness

Admin Users

Moderation capabilities, system stability

Supervisor

(Faculty)

Academic requirements, project scope adherence

Examiners (Faculty)

Documentation completeness, academic standards

Design Concerns by the stakeholders are valid and must be taken with extreme care lying within solid standards of a fully functional and reliable software. Usability at which providing a user-friendly navigation so a person could join the team in 3 clicks aligning. Ensuring a responsive design on both Desktop and Mobile web based scales by responsive design. A reliable and stable core functions cooperating with error handling functions. Security is extremely important in our scope since the system should have access to user location, achieving a solid user and data security authentication.

  1. Context Viewpoint

  1. Design Concerns

    The Context Viewpoint is the fundamental perspective which characterizes the GameOn Sports Team Matching Application as a black box and pays attention to its relations with the outside world instead of to its internal execution. This viewpoint is mainly aimed at:

  • List Key Actors and Roles of Actors -The system deals with several actors, they include:
  • Players (host/player) users who can create a profile, play in teams and take part in creating the sport events.

  • Administrators which are in charge of moderating content, managing reports and user accounts.
  • External Services :
  • Google Maps API  (matching near teams)
  • DeepSeek AI API (create individual training suggestions)
  • Core services :
  • User Management including registration, customisation of profiles, and authentication.
  • Team Formation & Matching ; Allowing users to create teams or join a team according to skill and location.
  • AI-Empowered Training Suggestions using DeepSeek API to provide individualized sports performance recommendations.
  • Moderation & Reporting gives the users the ability to report inappropriate materials, and the moderation is monitored by the admin.
  • Administrative Services: Managing user accounts, deleting teams upon the breach of the policy, and platform management

  1. Design View

 It is vital for the use cases to precisely reflect the core features and requirements. each core feature should be converted into its conceptual design.

Key features demonstrated through the UseCase diagrams ( referring to SRS document)

  1. Player Interactions
  • Register and manage User Profiles ( UC01 , UC02 )
  • Create, edit, and delete games ( UC03, UC04, UC05 , UC06, UC07)  
  • Search and join teams ( UC10, UC09 )
  • Receive AI-based training Tips (UC14)
  1. Admin Interactions
  • Delete inappropriate flagged games (UC15)
  • View reports done flagged by users (UC16)
  1. External Features Integrations
  • Google Maps API  powering location based searches (UC10)
  • Deepseek AI API providing tips for enhancing performance (UC14)

Figure 2.1: Use Case Diagram for <GameOn sports team matching>

  1. Composition Viewpoint

         Since we are using the MVC The system is to be composed into different modules, each module is set to have their own functioning layers of View, Controllers and Models. While the Use Case diagram is not modularized, the reasoning behind a non-modularized Use case diagram g functions among what might seem as different modules. In this Section we are going to break down the system into modules that are internally functioning and externally communicating with other modules.

  1. Design Concerns

The design aspect of each model should be maintained to process information internally and have an external communication with other modules. The first module is the user module which is responsible for registering new users, logging in users and providing user  information for other modules.

The second module is the Game module, which controls all game related use cases from game information to managing game members and join requests to games as well as joining games requests.

 The third module is the Report module, it includes all the report related functions from the user aspect creating the reports to the admin reviewing reports. The report handles sending data to Game modules.  

The fourth module is the AI chatting module, Since we are not constructing our own NLP model and using an external API to generate the customized performance tips. The AI module is responsible for handling responses and requests back and forth from the API endpoint. The module is communicating with the user controller to save the API responses to the user accounts.

  1. Design View

GameOn Sports Team Matching system is structured as four interrelated subsystems that collaborate to provide full functionality of sports team matching.

The User Module is the central point of the system, where user authentication, user profile and role-based access control are handled. It has its own specific user types such as Player, Admin, and Host components where Player derives under the base User component. This subsystem offers key services to the users that are relied upon by other modules to verify users and profile data.

The Game Subsystem manages all operations of the game such as creation, management and forming teams. Game and Team components in this subsystem collaborate to enable organization of sports events and matching participants. This module uses the User Module to obtain player data and check user permission to perform game-related actions.

The Report Module deals with the complaint and feedback system where users can make the reports and administrators can review it. This subsystem is connected to the User and Game modules to collect the necessary context of the reports and make it possible to track and resolve the incidents comprehensively.

The External Interface Subsystem completely separates all third party service integrations with the core business logic. It handles the DeepSeek API to perform AI-driven performance analysis and Google Maps API to provide location-based services. This architecture will guarantee that the external service dependencies are all within one subsystem, isolating the core application to changes in third-party providers.

The modular architecture facilitates loose coupling by the use of good interfaces that enable the subsystems to work in isolation and share required data via standardized communication channels. This architecture facilitates scaling, simpler maintenance, and deployment flexibility as the system scales.

 

Figure 3.1: Component Diagram of <Name of the System>

  1. Logical Viewpoint

  1. Design Concerns

The logical perspective will be to expound on how the classes and their relationships are to be implemented in the gaming platform system. In this perspective, the conceptual model can be seen to be converted into tangible class structures and their interrelationships.

The system is based on the layered architecture where there is a strict division between entity classes and controller classes. The entity classes are the main business objects such as User, Game, Team, JoinRequest, and Reports whereas controller classes are the classes where the business logic and user interactions take place. This architecture is maintainable and is object-oriented.

The inheritance is applied to the class structure by using an abstract User class as a base to the Player, Host, and Admin classes to share functionality but still permit role-specific behavior. The class relationships facilitate the main functionality of the gaming platform, which is user management, game creation, team formation, and request processing using clear associations and dependencies.

  1. Design View

 The class diagram shows the entire system architecture that is divided into two main layers as entity classes and controller classes. The entity layer has the key business objects whereby User is the abstract base class of Player, Host, and Admin, which are inheriting the common attributes though they still have their own roles. Game class is the center of the system, and it is linked to the Team, JoinRequest, and Reports classes by several associations.

The controller layer is combined with the entity classes smoothly to execute business logic and user activities. RegisterController is in charge of registration and authentication of users, JoinRequestController is in charge of requesting to join a game, GameController is in charge of managing games and participants, TeamController is in charge of team creation, and ReportController is in charge of the reporting system. Every controller class relies on and communicates with their respective entity classes, which allows the proper separation of concerns, but still keeps the system coherent.

The integration enables clean separation of data representation (entities) and business logic (controllers) and so the system is easier to maintain and scale and can support all the core functionalities of a gaming platform.

Figure 4.3: Class diagram for <<GameOn Sports team matching>>

  1. Information Viewpoint

  1. Design Concerns

Major data or system objects are loaded into a relational database called GamingPlatformDB with 7 entities which are taken to represent the main system object or the business objects of the gaming platform system. The information domain is converted to normalized relational tables which preserve data integrity and allow efficient operation of querying processes. Primary and foreign key relationship provides sanity in organizing data in a manner that has referential integrity throughout data entities.

Entity Name and Description

Entity Name

Description

User

Base entity representing all system users

Player

A specialized user entity for players participating in games. referencing the user table

Host

A specialized user entity for users who created a game. referencing the user table

Game

Core entity representing Gaming sessions with all game-related information and status.

Team

Entity representing team formation within a game.

JoinRequest

Entity managing players requests to join specific games.

Reports

Entity for handling inappropriate behavior.

  1. Design View

4.5.2.1 System Entities (Alphabetical Order)

Entity

Type

Description

Game

Class/Object

Central gaming session entity containing all game-related information

Host

Class/Object

Specialized user entity who can create and manage gaming sessions

JoinRequest

Class/Object

Request entity for players wanting to join specific games

Player

Class/Object

Specialized user entity who participates in gaming sessions

Reports

Class/Object

Reporting entity for managing inappropriate behavior incidents

Team

Class/Object

Team formation entity within games for organizing participants

User

Class/Object

Base entity for all user types with common attributes

Entity: Game

Attribute Name

Type

Description

Maximum number of teams allowed

Description

Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD)

Figure 5.0 ERD

4.6  Interface Viewpoint

4.6.1 Design Concerns

The interface viewpoint encompasses user interface, hardware interface and software interface to provide uninterrupted communication with the system of the gaming platform to its user. The system also offers user-friendly web-based tools where users can register, view games, make join inclinations, create groups, and even report poor practices. Its users can communicate with the system using responsive web forms, dynamically updated game listing and the real time status updates and messaging systems to produce instant feedback on all the actions in the system.

4.6.2 Design View

4.6.2.1 User Interfaces

a) Logical Characteristics

The GameOn system utilizes a web based interface architecture with the following logical characteristics :

  1. Screen Formats and Layouts:

  • Registration Form: A multi-step form with user credentials validated as well as user details and selection of the skill level
  • Game Dashboard: Series of squares showing available games followed by filtering option with reference to location, level of skill and type of game
  • Game Details Page: The complete overview of a game, including game details, lists of participants, formation of teams, and join request information
  • User Profile Management: Game personal information, game history, and preferences are available through the tabbed interface of updating user profile information
  • Join Request Interface: Modal dialogs to receive request to join and real time status of approval
  • Game Management Panel: Real-time interface representing the game composition as an area to drag and drop by the host.
  • Reporting Interface: Tabular layout having properly pop up boxes and text fields to write description of incidents


  1. Navigation Structure  :
  • Primary navigation bar navigating to different pages ( Home , Upcoming Games, Hosted Games , Notification list, AI coach , profile )

  1. Report and Data Display:  
  • Listing of games with column sort and paging
  • Real time participant and availability counters
  • Join request status ( pending , approved , rejected )
  • Notifications page displaying system messages

        

b) Interface Optimization

  1. Usability Requirements:
  • Response Time: All user actions must receive visual feedback within 2 seconds
  • Error Handling: Clear, actionable error messages with suggested solutions
  1. Performance Criteria:
  • New users can complete registration process in under 5 minutes
  • Experienced users can browse and join games within 3 clicks from dashboard
  • search and filter operations return results within 1 second
  1.  Visual Design Standards
  • Consistante color scheme
  • Consistante buttons style
  • Success/error state visual feedback with appropriate iconography

4.6.2.2 Hardware Interfaces

  1. User Devices:
  • Supported Devices: Desktop computers, laptops, tablets, and smartphones.
  • Configuration Characteristics:
  • Screen Resolution: Responsive design supports resolutions from 320px (mobile) to 1920px (desktop).
  • Input Methods: Touchscreen (for mobile/tablet) and keyboard/mouse (for desktop/laptop).
  • Ports: No specific port requirements; communication is handled via HTTP/HTTPS protocols.
  1. GPS/Geolocation Hardware:
  • Supported Devices: GPS-enabled smartphones and tablets.
  • Configuration Characteristics:
  • Accuracy: Relies on device GPS hardware for location accuracy (typically within 5-10 meters).
  • Protocols: Communicates via Google Maps API for geolocation services.
  1. Servers:
  • Hosting Environment: Cloud-based servers (e.g., AWS, Vercel).
  • Configuration Characteristics:
  • Ports: Standard HTTP (80) and HTTPS (443) ports for web traffic.
  • Instruction Sets: x86-64 architecture for backend processing.
  1. Networking Hardware:
  • Routers/Switches: Standard networking equipment supporting TCP/IP protocols.
  • Configuration Characteristics:
  • Bandwidth: Minimum 10 Mbps for smooth operation.
  • Latency: <100ms for real-time features like team matching and notifications.

4.6.2.3 Software Interfaces

The GameOn system interfaces with the following software components:

  1. Operating System:
  • Name: Any modern OS (Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS).
  • Purpose: Provides the runtime environment for the application.
  1. Database Management System:
  • Name: MongoDB.
  • Mnemonic: MongoDB.
  • Version Number: 6.0 or later.
  • Source: MongoDB Inc.
  • Purpose: Stores and manages user profiles, team data, and game information.
  1. Backend Framework:
  • Name: Node.js with Express.js.
  • Mnemonic: Node/Express.
  • Version Number: Node.js v18 or later, Express.js v4.17 or later.
  • Source: OpenJS Foundation.
  • Purpose: Handles server-side logic, API endpoints, and business processes.
  1. Frontend Framework:
  • Name: Next.js.
  • Mnemonic: Next.
  • Version Number: 13.0 or later.
  • Source: Vercel.
  • Purpose: Provides server-side rendering and static site generation for the user interface.
  1. External APIs:
  • Google Maps API:
  • Purpose: Provides geolocation services for team and player discovery.
  • Interface: RESTful API with JSON payloads.
  • Documentation: Google Maps Platform Documentation.
  • DeepSeek API:
  • Purpose: Delivers AI-powered training tips and performance recommendations.
  • Interface: RESTful API with JSON payloads.
  • Documentation: DeepSeek NLP API.
  1. Authentication Service:
  • Name: JSON Web Tokens (JWT).
  • Mnemonic: JWT.
  • Version Number: N/A (standard protocol).
  • Source: Auth0.
  • Purpose: Secures user authentication and session management.

Communication Interfaces

The GameOn system utilizes the following communication protocols and interfaces:

  1. HTTP/HTTPS:
  • Purpose: Standard protocol for client-server communication.
  • Details: All frontend-backend interactions use HTTPS for encrypted data transmission.
  1. RESTful API:
  • Purpose: Facilitates communication between the frontend and backend.
  • Interface: JSON-based payloads for requests and responses.
  • Endpoints: Defined in the backend (Node.js/Express.js) for user management, team operations, etc.
  1. WebSockets:
  • Purpose: Enables real-time notifications (e.g., team join requests, match updates).
  • Interface: Socket.io library for bidirectional communication.
  1. Google Maps API:
  • Protocol: HTTPS.
  • Purpose: Fetches location data and displays maps for geolocation-based searches.
  1. Database Communication:
  • Protocol: TCP/IP.
  • Purpose: Connects the backend to MongoDB for data persistence.
  1. External Service APIs:
  • Protocol: HTTPS.
  • Purpose: Integrates with third-party services like DeepSeek AI for NLP features.

4.7 Structure Viewpoint

4.7.1 Design Concerns

The Structure Viewpoint discusses basic architecture issues of the GameOn system. It is mainly aimed at defining the modular structure of the system and patterns of interaction and at maintaining compatibility with the fundamental principles of software engineering. The diagram in particular addresses three important design issues: First, it defines logical boundaries between subsystems to ensure separation of concerns, so that user management, gameplay and reporting are not tightly coupled.

Second, we establish navigation visibility rules which regulate the interaction of packages with each other like limiting direct access of external services to user entities. Third, it hides the technicalities of implementation to provide a domain-centric perspective that can be understood by technical and non-technical stakeholders. This will make the architecture maintainable with well defined modular roles and be able to scale up in the future, especially in the incorporation of other external services or the increase in the number of user roles.

4.7.2 Design View

The MVC architectural vision is demonstrated in the package diagram below with four interconnected subsystems. The User Management subsystem focuses the representation of actors on Admin, User, Host and Player classes, providing the core of identity and access control in the system. Next to this, the Game Subsystem encloses gameplay domain logic by interrelated Game, Team, and JoinRequest classes, which are intentionally separated into user management to enable separate development.

Moderation issues are addressed using a single Report class in a minimalist Report Subsystem, which shows the flexibility of the architecture to support complex and simple functional areas. External Services are presented as a separate package, which underlines the fact that they are interchangeable parts, but not the fundamental system components.

Dependency arrows express interaction rules: User-to-Game connections imply creation rights, Host-to-Game connections imply ownership rights, and Player-to-Team connections imply participation processes. More importantly, all external service dependencies are rooted in the Game Subsystem, and this creates an architectural firewall that does not allow API to be coupled with user or report modules.

Figure 5.1: Package Diagram for <GameOn sports>

4.8 Interaction Viewpoint

4.8.1 Design Concerns

The Interaction Viewpoint deals with the way in which MVC elements interact in order to achieve the system functions via regulated processes. There is a total of 17 sequence diagrams illustrates three major architectural principles: First, a strong layer isolation so that Views only communicate with Controllers through HTTP requests, but never with Models. Second, transactional consistency is ensured by doing all data validation and business logic in controllers prior to model interactions as in the case of UC01 (Registration) where AuthController is used to validate inputs prior to UserModel persisting data. Third, model-layer coupling is avoided by external service integrations (e.g. DeepSeek API in UC14) being mediated by controllers. The given approach addresses the key interaction issues: it normalizes error handling (e.g., validation fail flows in UC05), role-based access (e.g., game deletion, only by an admin in UC15), and API calls (e.g., geo-search, coordinated in UC10, with Google Maps).

4.8.2 Design View

The system is developed in such a way that it has three key components which complement each other. The first ones are the user interface screens - these are the screens that people see and use, such as registration forms or game listings. When a person enters information or presses a button, the action is transmitted to the middle processing layer. This intermediate layer is a traffic controller, verifying all the data, making decisions, and organizing what should be done next. It may store data, connect to special services such as location maps, or check permissions.

The data management component is the part that is behind the scenes and does all the information storage and retrieval in collaboration with the database. This three-part format is applied throughout the system. As an example, when a person creates an account, his or her data is verified and stored. The system automatically checks the authority of taking such actions as creating games or removing players. Other administrative functions such as management of reports are also in the same orderly manner and thus, there is proper management.

The MVC allows for seamless interface operation, the middle layer does all the complicated decision making and the data component stores the information safely. Separating these responsibilities but maintaining a strong connection between them, the system is both flexible to future enhancements and reliable to use on a daily basis. This design fits well with the rest of the system structure depicted in other diagrams.

  1. SD01: Registration

4.9 State Dynamic Viewpoint

4.9.1 Design Concerns

The State Dynamic Viewpoint describes the dynamics of the system responding to the actions of the user and internal situation. In this view, the emphasis is on the progression of core entities (such as user accounts, game sessions, join requests, and moderation reports) through their operational lifecycles. Every entity moves between the controlled states according to the predetermined triggers, e.g. the player provides the registration information or the host accepts a team member. Four crucial issues that are directly covered by the design are lifecycle management, which determines the overall flow of entities, such as games (from their creation to their completion); event handling, which specifies how to react to events, such as a cancellation or an approval; error recovery, which allows providing fallbacks in case of invalid operations, such as trying to join a full game; and role-based control, as players, hosts, and administrators have different workflows.

This perspective is valuable to various stakeholders: developers look to it when writing code to handle the state transition logic, and testers use the same perspective to ensure that all possible transition paths are tested, and designers use the same perspective to ensure that all workflows produce consistent experiences. Capturing these dynamic behaviors formally, the diagram set makes sure that the system can deal with both expected and exceptional cases in a predictable way and that the user roles and the system responsibilities are kept strictly separate.

4.9.2 Design View

UserRegistration

Figure 5.1 State Machine Diagram for User Registration

GameCreat

Figure 5.2 State Machine Diagram for Game Creation

JoinRequest

Figure 5.3 State Machine Diagram for JoinRequestApproval

Report

Figure 5.4 State Machine Diagram for Reports

4.10 Algorithm Viewpoint (DECISION TABLE)

4.10.1 Design Concerns

From an algorithmic perspective, the decision tables demonstrate an effective structured approach to conditional logic processing. Each table operates as an optimized truth table with O(1) rule identification, eliminating the need for cascading if-else structures that could degrade to O(n) complexity. The binary condition framework (T/F/-) creates a clean state space that enables efficient bitwise operations and parallel condition evaluation.

The design successfully handles multiple decision paths through a systematic rule-based approach, where each rule represents a unique execution path. The "don't care" (-) notation provides algorithmic flexibility by reducing the total number of required rules while maintaining logical completeness. This approach scales well for the current use cases, with each table maintaining manageable complexity levels.

The modular separation of decision tables allows for independent optimization of each functional area, supporting maintainable code generation and efficient rule engine implementations. This structure facilitates both manual verification and automated testing of business logic paths.

5.0 User Interface Design

5.1 Overview of User Interface

5.1.2 Game Management and Registration of Users

The first step is to sign-up to the gaming site where the user makes a basic personal profile that entails such details as the athletic skill level and the user location preference. Upon registration, users are allowed to search through the available games, which have been classified by location, level of expertise, and kind of the game. The host users also enjoy more capabilities to generate new game sessions by asserting the details about a game including the entry, location, the number of teams allowed, the number of seats available and the level of skills needed. The system also issues real time update of game availability, seat-availability as well as registration status so that any user will have an updated information when deciding whether to participate or not.

5.1.2 Team Formation and Get Joining Request Process

Games whose level of difficulty and general preference by a player are met can be joined with the game whose requirements are verified automatically by the system shutting down the join request whenever there is a claim of unqualified participation due to skills required and presence of vacancies. The platform supports confirmation of the request (pending, approved, or defeated) and informs the user about changes in the status in time. After approval, they are made into teams with definite signs of team capacity and current membership being given. Host users are able to control participants approval, check the balance in the team, and adjust everything to have a fair game organization.

5.1.3 Report and Game Administration

The system has a reporting system where a user can report unacceptable conduct by giving reasons and providing descriptions of the nature which it falls in. The users get confirmation of submission of reports and they can track the status of resolution. During the gaming process, the users get updated notices concerning the changes in the game status, game update, and other announcements. The platform includes transparency (demonstrating game statuses: open, full, closed, indicates the number of participants, compositions of teams), but, at the same time, present hosts with the opportunity to regulate registration periods, end games when needed, make the whole process of gaming as comfortable as possible.

  1. Overview

This document is a detailed design for test cases, Which allow for standard testing according to software quality assurance principles. Each test case is driven from it’s corresponding use case. While test cases have many scenarios based on the context of probable interactions of users with core features.

The targeted audience are the end users and the user whom accepted to be on the user acceptance testing (UAT)

Appendices

A

Traceability Matrix

  1. Introduction

  1. Purpose

This Software Testing Documentation (STD) presents the testing plan and techniques applied to the game match applications within the GameOn Web-based portal to interface with sports team enthusiasts within the environment of the specific skill level and location-based desire. This document contains the test cases, test scenarios and methods of validating both functional and non-functional requirements within the Software Requirements Specification (SRS). Even though this STD involves descriptions of tests and test procedures, it does not involve test results, since that is part of the scope of the software engineering course. The potential readers of this document will be the developer, the quality assurance tester, project supervisors which are Dr. Rohayanti Hassan, the stakeholder and also the end-user of the project.

  1. Scope

The GameOn app allows the user to generate a profile, the creation and management of teams, the ability to search for the nearest players or teams, and the provision of recommendations to train through AI. The main functionalities that will be tested will provide a user with the opportunity to register, create and customize teams, run searches, and manage administration options. The systems with direct booking and payment processing within the venue will be out of the scope of the project and excluded in testing. The key purposes of the testing process are ensuring the achievement of all the functional requirements, the system should be usable and predictable, and it has to work safely on various web browsers.

  1. Definitions, Acronyms and Abbreviation

AI: Artificial Intelligence

API: Application Programming Interface

GPS: Global Positioning System

JWT: JSON Web Token

UI/UX: User Interface/User Experience

SQA : software quality assurance

  1. System Overview

STD has been designed to deliver an extensive testing structure of the GameOn application. It starts with the test cases made based on the SRS use cases (UC01-UC17) which involve functional and non-functional aspects of the system, e.g. user registration and team management, and usability and security. Test scenarios provide the detailed workflow description, i.e., searching by team by skill level or reporting of unwanted content. The browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari), as well as the database (MongoDB), are listed as the test environment requirements. Limitations, like limitations to use free-tier APIs, and also manual testing, are also mentioned. In later parts, the test cases will be presented in detail to every use case covering a thorough verification of the system functionality and performance.

Actual Result

Pass/Fail

TC006_02_01

User not game owner

Access denied error

TC006_02_02

Member not in game

Member not found error

TC006_02_03

User cancels removal

Member not removed

TC006_02_04

Try to remove self (game owner)

Cannot remove owner error

TC006_02_05

Remove member from completed game

Cannot modify completed game error

TC006_02_06

Network error during removal

Network error, retry option

TC006_02_07

Member already removed

Member not found error

TC006_02_08

Database error during removal

Database error message

TC006_02_09

Invalid member ID

Invalid member ID error

TC006_02_10

Session expired during removal

Session timeout error

TC006_02_11

Game locked for editing

Game modification locked error

TC006_02_12

Remove member during game

Cannot remove during active game

Input Data

Expected Result

Actual Result

Input Data

Expected Result

Actual Result

Pass/Fail

Input Data

Expected Result

Actual Result

TC017_02_08

Extremely large number of notifications (1000+)

Paginated display with loading indicators

3.0 Test Approach Analysis

INTRODUCTION

This test approach analysis provides a detailed testing plan of the GameOn Sports Team Matching system with nine important use cases including user registration and game management. The testing methodologies such as Equivalence Partitioning (EP) and Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) are used to provide adequate validation of the system functionality in the analysis. Every use case has been broken down into specific test scenarios to test both valid and invalid input scenarios, especially in security weaknesses, load testing, and edge cases. The strategy involves 200+ separate test cases that include user authentication, profile management, game creation and administration, membership management, and join request processing. Particular emphasis is placed on the real-life situations, including simultaneous access by multiple users, system crashes, and malicious input attempts. This systematic approach guarantees the coverage of functional requirements in detail as well as being user-focused, data integrity, and system reliability in any operational context.

UC01: Register User - Equivalence Partitioning (EP) and Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)

Table 1.1: EP and BVA for UC01: Register User

Test Case Field

EP Classes

BVA Values

TC001_01 Email

• EP Class 1 (valid): Email with valid format (e.g., "john.doe@email.com")

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Email already exists (e.g., existing email)

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Email without '@' (e.g., "invalid-email")

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Email without prefix (e.g., "@email.com")

• EP Class 5 (invalid): Email without domain (e.g., "user@")

• EP Class 6 (invalid): Email with double dots (e.g., "user..double@email.com")

• EP Class 7 (invalid): Email with spaces (e.g., "user name@email.com")

• "a@b.co" (minimum valid - valid)

• "john.doe@email.com" (valid)

• "very.long.email.address.test@domain.com" (long email - valid)

• "" (blank - invalid)

• "@email.com" (invalid)

• "user@" (invalid)

• "invalid-email" (invalid)

• "user..double@email.com" (invalid)

• 1000+ character email (invalid)

TC001_02 Password

• EP Class 1 (valid): Password with uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and special characters (e.g., "SecurePass123!")

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Password too short (e.g., "Pass1")

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Password without uppercase (e.g., "password123")

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Password without lowercase (e.g., "PASSWORD123")

• EP Class 5 (invalid): Password without numbers (e.g., "Password")

• EP Class 6 (invalid): Password without special characters (e.g., "Password123")

• EP Class 7 (invalid): Password too weak (e.g., "password")

• EP Class 8 (invalid): SQL injection attempt (e.g., "' OR '1'='1")

• EP Class 9 (invalid): XSS attempt (e.g., "<script>alert('xss')</script>")

• "MinPass1!" (minimum valid - valid)

• "SecurePass123!" (valid)

• "Pass1" (too short - invalid)

• "" (blank - invalid)

• "123" (invalid)

• "password" (invalid)

• "PASSWORD123" (invalid)

• "Password" (invalid)

• "' OR '1'='1" (invalid)

• "<script>alert('xss')</script>" (invalid)

TC001_03 Sports Selection

• EP Class 1 (valid): Single sport with skill level (e.g., "Basketball - Beginner")

• EP Class 2 (valid): Multiple sports with different skills (e.g., "Basketball - Advanced, Soccer - Intermediate")

• EP Class 3 (valid): No sports selected (optional field)

• EP Class 4 (valid): All skill levels (Beginner, Intermediate, Advanced, Expert)

• "Basketball - Beginner" (valid)

• "Soccer - Intermediate" (valid)

• "Tennis - Advanced" (valid)

• "Football - Expert" (valid)

• Multiple sports selection (valid)

• No selection (valid - optional)

TC001_04 Overall

• Combines all field validations to test complete registration flow

• All valid inputs (successful registration)

• Various combinations of invalid inputs (registration failures)

2. UC02: Edit Profile - Equivalence Partitioning (EP) and Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)

Table 2.1: EP and BVA for UC02: Edit Profile

Test Case Field

EP Classes

BVA Values

TC002_01 Full Name

• EP Class 1 (valid): Name with letters and spaces (e.g., "John Smith")

• EP Class 2 (valid): Name with special characters (e.g., "María González")

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Name with numbers (e.g., "John123")

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Name with special symbols (e.g., "@#$%^&*()")

• EP Class 5 (invalid): Blank name

• "A" (1 character - boundary)

• "John Smith" (valid)

• "María González" (special chars - valid)

• 50 characters exactly (boundary - valid)

• "" (blank - invalid)

• "John123" (invalid)

• "@#$%^&*()" (invalid)

TC002_02 Location

• EP Class 1 (valid): Standard location (e.g., "New York")

• EP Class 2 (valid): International location (e.g., "São Paulo, Brazil")

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Location too long (1000+ characters)

• "Chicago" (valid)

• "São Paulo, Brazil" (international - valid)

• 1000+ characters (invalid)

TC002_03 Age

• EP Class 1 (valid): Valid age range (e.g., 18-100)

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Negative age (e.g., -5)

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Age too high (e.g., 150)

• 18 (minimum valid - valid)

• 25 (valid)

• 100 (maximum valid - valid)

• -5 (invalid)

• 150 (invalid)

TC002_04 Phone Number

• EP Class 1 (valid): Valid phone format (e.g., "+1-555-123-4567")

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Invalid phone format (e.g., "123")

• "+1-555-123-4567" (valid)

• "123" (invalid)

TC002_05 Bio

• EP Class 1 (valid): Normal bio length (e.g., "Love playing sports on weekends")

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Bio too long (10000+ characters)

• "Love playing sports" (valid)

• Character limit exactly (boundary - valid)

• 10000+ characters (invalid)

TC002_06 Profile Picture

• EP Class 1 (valid): Valid image file (JPG, PNG)

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Invalid file type (e.g., virus.exe)

• EP Class 3 (invalid): File too large (50MB)

• 1KB image (minimum - valid)

• Standard image (valid)

• Maximum allowed size (boundary - valid)

• virus.exe (invalid)

• 50MB file (invalid)

3. UC03: Create Games - Equivalence Partitioning (EP) and Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)

Table 3.1: EP and BVA for UC03: Create Games

Test Case Field

EP Classes

BVA Values

TC003_01 Game Name

• EP Class 1 (valid): Standard game name (e.g., "Sunday Basketball")

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Blank game name

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Game name too long (1000+ characters)

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Game name with special characters (e.g., "@#$%^&*()")

• EP Class 5 (valid): Duplicate game name (warning only)

• "Game" (minimum valid - valid)

• "Sunday Basketball" (valid)

• Maximum character limit (boundary - valid)

• "" (blank - invalid)

• 1000+ characters (invalid)

• "@#$%^&*()" (invalid)

TC003_02 Sport Selection

• EP Class 1 (valid): Standard sports (Basketball, Soccer, Tennis, etc.)

• EP Class 2 (invalid): No sport selected

• "Basketball" (valid)

• "Soccer" (valid)

• "Tennis" (valid)

• All available sports (valid)

• "" (blank - invalid)

TC003_03 Location

• EP Class 1 (valid): Standard location (e.g., "Central Park")

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Blank location

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Invalid location format

• "Park" (minimum valid - valid)

• "Central Park" (valid)

• "Santa Monica Beach" (valid)

• "" (blank - invalid)

TC003_04 Player Capacity

• EP Class 1 (valid): Normal capacity (e.g., 10 players)

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Zero capacity

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Capacity too large (1000)

• 1 (minimum valid - valid)

• 10 (valid)

• Maximum allowed (boundary - valid)

• 0 (invalid)

• 1000 (invalid)

TC003_05 Date & Time

• EP Class 1 (valid): Future date and time

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Past date and time

• EP Class 3 (valid): Current date and time (boundary)

• Today + 1 day (valid)

• Future date (valid)

• Yesterday (invalid)

• Current time (boundary)

TC003_06 Skill Level

• EP Class 1 (valid): All skill levels (Beginner, Intermediate, Advanced, All Levels)

• "Beginner" (valid)

• "Intermediate" (valid)

• "Advanced" (valid)

• "All Levels" (valid)

4. UC04: Manage Game - Equivalence Partitioning (EP) and Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)

Table 4.1: EP and BVA for UC04: Manage Game

Test Case Field

EP Classes

BVA Values

TC004_01 User Authorization

• EP Class 1 (valid): Game owner

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Non-owner user

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Not logged in user

• Game owner (valid)

• Regular member (invalid)

• Non-member (invalid)

• Not logged in (invalid)

TC004_02 Game Status

• EP Class 1 (valid): Active game

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Completed game

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Cancelled game

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Non-existent game

• Active game (valid)

• Completed game (invalid)

• Cancelled game (boundary)

• Deleted game (invalid)

TC004_03 Member List Size

• EP Class 1 (valid): Normal member count (1-50)

• EP Class 2 (valid): Large member count (100+)

• EP Class 3 (valid): Maximum member count (500+)

• 1 member (minimum - valid)

• 50 members (valid)

• 100+ members (performance test)

• 500+ members (stress test)

5. UC06: Remove Game Members - Equivalence Partitioning (EP) and Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)

Table 5.1: EP and BVA for UC06: Remove Game Members

Test Case Field

EP Classes

BVA Values

TC006_01 Member Selection

• EP Class 1 (valid): Existing game member

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Non-existent member

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Already removed member

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Game owner (self-removal)

• Valid member (valid)

• Multiple members (valid)

• Non-member (invalid)

• Game owner (invalid)

• Already removed (invalid)

TC006_02 Removal Reason

• EP Class 1 (valid): Reason provided

• EP Class 2 (valid): No reason provided

• EP Class 3 (valid): Standard reasons (inappropriate behavior, non-payment, etc.)

• "Inappropriate behavior" (valid)

• "Non-payment" (valid)

• "" (blank - valid)

• Custom reason (valid)

TC006_03 Game State

• EP Class 1 (valid): Game not started

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Game in progress

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Game completed

• Scheduled game (valid)

• Game in progress (invalid)

• Completed game (invalid)

6. UC08: Accept Join Requests - Equivalence Partitioning (EP) and Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)

Table 6.1: EP and BVA for UC08: Accept Join Requests

Test Case Field

EP Classes

BVA Values

TC008_01 Request Status

• EP Class 1 (valid): Pending request

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Already processed request

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Invalid/expired request

• New pending request (valid)

• Multiple pending requests (valid)

• Processed request (invalid)

• Expired request (invalid)

TC008_02 Game Capacity

• EP Class 1 (valid): Game has available spots

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Game is full

• EP Class 3 (valid): Game at capacity - 1 (boundary)

• Game with 5/10 spots (valid)

• Game with 9/10 spots (boundary - valid)

• Game with 10/10 spots (invalid)

TC008_03 Player Eligibility

• EP Class 1 (valid): Player meets requirements

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Player doesn't meet skill requirements

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Blocked player

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Suspended player

• Qualified player (valid)

• Under-skilled player (invalid)

• Over-skilled player (boundary)

• Blocked player (invalid)

• Suspended player (invalid)

7. UC09: Send Join Request - Equivalence Partitioning (EP) and Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)

Table 7.1: EP and BVA for UC09: Send Join Request

Test Case Field

EP Classes

BVA Values

TC009_01 User Status

• EP Class 1 (valid): Logged in user

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Not logged in user

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Already game member

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Previous request pending

• Logged in user (valid)

• Not logged in (invalid)

• Current member (invalid)

• Pending request exists (invalid)

TC009_02 Game Availability

• EP Class 1 (valid): Game accepting members

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Game full

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Registration deadline passed

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Game not accepting members

• Open game (valid)

• Game with 1 spot left (boundary - valid)

• Full game (invalid)

• Closed registration (invalid)

TC009_03 Message Content

• EP Class 1 (valid): Request with message

• EP Class 2 (valid): Request without message

• EP Class 3 (valid): Request with detailed information

• "Excited to join!" (valid)

• "" (blank - valid)

• Detailed message (valid)

• Message at character limit (boundary)

TC009_04 Skill Requirements

• EP Class 1 (valid): User meets skill requirements

• EP Class 2 (invalid): User doesn't meet skill requirements

• Beginner requesting beginner game (valid)

• Advanced requesting intermediate game (valid)

• Beginner requesting advanced game (invalid)

TC010: Search Games (UC010)

Table 3.2: EP and BVA for TC010: Search Games (UC010)

Test Case Field

EP Classes

BVA Values

TC010_01 Sport Selection

• EP Class 1 (valid): Valid sport from dropdown (e.g., "Football", "Basketball")

• EP Class 2 (valid): Multiple sports selected

• EP Class 3 (valid): No sport selected (all sports)

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Invalid sport name

• "Football" (valid)

• "Basketball" (valid)

• ["Football", "Basketball"] (multiple - valid)

• "" (no selection - valid)

• "InvalidSport" (invalid)

TC010_02 Location

• EP Class 1 (valid): Valid city name (e.g., "New York")

• EP Class 2 (valid): Valid state/region

• EP Class 3 (valid): Blank location (search all)

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Special characters in location

• EP Class 5 (invalid): Numbers in location name

• "New York" (valid city)

• "California" (valid state)

• "" (blank - valid for all locations)

• "City@#$" (special chars - invalid)

• "City123" (numbers - invalid)

• "A" (single char - boundary)

• "Very Long City Name Location" (long name - boundary)

TC010_03 Skill Level

• EP Class 1 (valid): Beginner

• EP Class 2 (valid): Intermediate

• EP Class 3 (valid): Advanced

• EP Class 4 (valid): No skill level selected

• EP Class 5 (invalid): Invalid skill level

• "Beginner" (valid)

• "Intermediate" (valid)

• "Advanced" (valid)

• "" (no selection - valid)

• "Expert" (invalid level)

TC010_04 Keywords

• EP Class 1 (valid): Single keyword (e.g., "friendly")

• EP Class 2 (valid): Multiple keywords

• EP Class 3 (valid): Blank keywords

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Special characters only

• EP Class 5 (invalid): Extremely long keywords

• EP Class 6 (invalid): SQL injection attempts

• "friendly" (single keyword - valid)

• "weekend match fun" (multiple - valid)

• "" (blank - valid)

• "@#$%^&*" (special chars only - invalid)

• "Very long keyword string that exceeds normal search limits and might cause system issues" (too long - invalid)

• "'; DROP TABLE games; --" (SQL injection - invalid)

3. TC012: Leave Game (UC012)

Table 3.3: EP and BVA for TC012: Leave Game (UC012)

Test Case Field

EP Classes

BVA Values

TC012_01 User Role

• EP Class 1 (valid): Regular participant

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Game host without host transfer

• EP Class 3 (invalid): User not in game

• EP Class 4 (invalid): User already left game

• Regular participant (valid)

• Game host (invalid without transfer)

• Non-participant (invalid)

• Already left user (invalid)

TC012_02 Game Status

• EP Class 1 (valid): Upcoming game

• EP Class 2 (valid): Open for registration

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Game in progress

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Completed game

• EP Class 5 (invalid): Cancelled game

• Upcoming game (valid)

• Open registration (valid)

• In progress (invalid)

• Completed (invalid)

• Cancelled (invalid)

TC012_03 Confirmation

• EP Class 1 (valid): User confirms leaving

• EP Class 2 (valid): User cancels leaving

• EP Class 3 (invalid): No response to confirmation

• "Confirm" (valid - proceed)

• "Cancel" (valid - stay in game)

• Timeout/No response (invalid)

13. TC013: Report Game (UC013)

Table 3.4: EP and BVA for TC013: Report Game (UC013)

Test Case Field

EP Classes

BVA Values

TC013_01 Report Reason

• EP Class 1 (valid): Inappropriate Content

• EP Class 2 (valid): Harassment

• EP Class 3 (valid): Spam

• EP Class 4 (valid): Fake Game

• EP Class 5 (invalid): No reason selected

• EP Class 6 (valid): Multiple reasons selected

• "Inappropriate Content" (valid)

• "Harassment" (valid)

• "Spam" (valid)

• "Fake Game" (valid)

• "" (no selection - invalid)

• ["Spam", "Inappropriate Content"] (multiple - valid)

TC013_02 Description

• EP Class 1 (valid): Detailed description (50-500 chars)

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Blank description

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Too short description (<10 chars)

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Too long description (>1000 chars)

• EP Class 5 (invalid): Special characters only

• EP Class 6 (invalid): Inappropriate content in description

• "Player using offensive language during game" (valid)

• "" (blank - invalid)

• "Bad" (too short - invalid)

• "A".repeat(1001) (too long - invalid)

• "@#$%^&*()_+" (special chars only - invalid)

• "This report contains inappropriate language..." (inappropriate - invalid)

• "Valid description" (minimum valid - 10 chars)

• "Valid description at boundary" (boundary - 500 chars)

14. TC014: Get AI Performance Tips (UC014)

Table 3.5: EP and BVA for TC014: Get AI Performance Tips (UC014)

Test Case Field

EP Classes

BVA Values

TC014_01 Sport Selection

• EP Class 1 (valid): Popular sports (Football, Basketball, Tennis)

• EP Class 2 (valid): Less common sports (Cricket, Rugby)

• EP Class 3 (valid): Individual sports (Swimming, Running)

• EP Class 4 (valid): Team sports

• EP Class 5 (invalid): Invalid sport name

• EP Class 6 (valid): No sport specified (general fitness)

• "Football" (popular sport - valid)

• "Cricket" (less common - valid)

• "Swimming" (individual - valid)

• "Basketball" (team sport - valid)

• "InvalidSport" (invalid)

• "" (no sport - valid for general tips)

TC014_02 Prompt Text

• EP Class 1 (valid): Specific skill improvement (20-200 chars)

• EP Class 2 (valid): General performance query

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Blank prompt

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Too short prompt (<5 chars)

• EP Class 5 (invalid): Too long prompt (>2000 chars)

• EP Class 6 (invalid): Inappropriate content

• EP Class 7 (invalid): Special characters only

• "Improve passing accuracy" (specific - valid)

• "How to get better at sports" (general - valid)

• "" (blank - invalid)

• "Help" (too short - invalid)

• "A".repeat(2001) (too long - invalid)

• "Inappropriate content here..." (inappropriate - invalid)

• "@#$%^" (special chars only - invalid)

• "Tips" (minimum - 5 chars boundary)

• "Valid prompt exactly 200 characters..." (200 chars boundary)

16. TC015: Delete Any Game (UC015) - Admin Function

Table 3.6: EP and BVA for TC015: Delete Any Game (UC015)

Test Case Field

EP Classes

BVA Values

TC015_01 User Role

• EP Class 1 (valid): Admin user

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Regular user

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Non-logged in user

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Suspended admin

• Admin (valid)

• Regular user (invalid)

• Guest/Not logged in (invalid)

• Suspended admin (invalid)

TC015_02 Game Status

• EP Class 1 (valid): Active game with violations

• EP Class 2 (valid): Reported game

• EP Class 3 (valid): Upcoming game

• EP Class 4 (invalid): Non-existent game

• EP Class 5 (invalid): Already deleted game

• EP Class 6 (valid): Completed game (for cleanup)

• Active reported game (valid)

• Multiple reports game (valid)

• Upcoming violating game (valid)

• Non-existent game ID (invalid)

• Already deleted game (invalid)

• Old completed game (valid for cleanup)

TC015_03 Confirmation

• EP Class 1 (valid): Admin confirms deletion

• EP Class 2 (valid): Admin cancels deletion

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Session timeout during confirmation

• "Confirm Delete" (valid - proceed)

• "Cancel" (valid - abort)

• Session timeout (invalid)

17. TC017: View Notifications (UC017)

Table 3.7: EP and BVA for TC017: View Notifications (UC017)

Test Case Field

EP Classes

BVA Values

TC017_01 User Login Status

• EP Class 1 (valid): Logged in user

• EP Class 2 (invalid): Not logged in user

• EP Class 3 (invalid): Expired session

• Logged in user (valid)

• Not logged in (invalid)

• Expired session (invalid)

TC017_02 Notification Count

• EP Class 1 (valid): 1-10 notifications

• EP Class 2 (valid): 11-50 notifications

• EP Class 3 (valid): 50+ notifications (pagination)

• EP Class 4 (valid): 0 notifications (empty state)

• EP Class 5 (boundary): Exactly at pagination limit

• 5 notifications (normal - valid)

• 25 notifications (medium - valid)

• 100 notifications (high - valid)

• 0 notifications (empty - valid)

• 50 notifications (pagination boundary)

• 1 notification (minimum - boundary)

• 1000 notifications (stress test - boundary)

TC017_03 Notification Types

• EP Class 1 (valid): Game acceptance notifications

• EP Class 2 (valid): Game deletion notifications

• EP Class 3 (valid): Join request notifications

• EP Class 4 (valid): System maintenance notifications

• EP Class 5 (valid): Mixed notification types

• EP Class 6 (invalid): Corrupted notification data

• "Join request accepted" (valid)

• "Game deleted by admin" (valid)

• "New join request" (valid)

• "System maintenance scheduled" (valid)

• Mixed types (valid)

• Corrupted data (invalid)

REFERENCES

Auth0. (2024). JWT authentication best practices. https://auth0.com/docs/secure/tokens/json-web-tokens

DeepSeek. (2024). Natural language processing for sports performance. https://deepseek.com/nlp-api

Google Cloud. (2024). Google Maps Platform documentation. https://cloud.google.com/maps-platform

Global Badminton Participation Report. 2018

https://bwfbadminton.com 

Medium. (2024). Connecting through sport: A UX case study. https://medium.com/design-bootcamp/connecting-through-sport-a-ux-case-study-a5f94ae18c1b

MongoDB Inc. (2024). Building scalable applications with NoSQL. https://www.mongodb.com/use-cases/scalability

Nielsen Norman Group. (2023). *The 3-click rule: Myth or usability guideline?* https://www.nngroup.com/articles/3-click-rule/
